coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of graphene quantum dots and carbodiimide in promoting resin–dentin bond durability

coredata.dc:description:
Objective Resin-based dental adhesion is mostly utilized in minimally invasive operative dentistry. However, improving the durability and stability of resin–dentin bond interfaces remain a challenge. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) reinforced by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were introduced to modify the resin–dentin bond interfaces, thereby promoting their durability and stability.
Methods GQDs, EDC, and EDC+GQDs groups were designed to evaluate the effects of GQDs and EDC on collagenase activity, the interaction of GQDs with collagen, and the resin–dentin interface. First, the effects of GQDs and EDC on collagenase activity was evaluated by Collagenase (EC 3.4.24.3) reacting with its substrate. The interaction of GQDs and EDC with collagen were evaluated by cross-linking degree analysis, sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and enzymatic hydrolysis. Second, the acid-etched and rinse adhesive system was used to evaluate the resin–dentin bond on the basis of microtensile bond strength, in situ zymography and fluorescence confocal laser scanning microscopy.
Results GQDs could inhibit collagenase activity. GQDs with the aid of EDC could cross-link collagen via covalent bonds and improve the anti-enzymatic hydrolysis of collagen. In the resin–dentin adhesion model, the μTBS of the EDC+GQDs group was significantly higher than the other control groups after thermocycling. The addition of EDC to GQDs could inhibit matrix metalloproteinase activity and promote the integrity of the bonding interfaces after thermocycling.
Significance This study presents a novel strategy to modify the resin–dentin interface and provides a new application for GQDs. This strategy has the potential to improve the durability of resin-based restoration in dentistry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SDS–PAGE bands of collagen modified by the cross-linker. From left to right: the four electrophoresis bands correspond to the control, EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs groups, respectively.
ATR-FTIR spectra of non-cross-linking and cross-linking collagen (EDC, GQDs, EDC+GQDs). From bottom to top: untreated collagen film (a), EDC (b), GQDs (c), and EDC+GQDs (d) modified collagen film.

CLSM fluorescence images showing the in situ MMP zymography of the HL (pointers) at resin–dentin interfaces of different groups after bonding for 24 h during distilled water storage (immediate bonding interface). (a) Control group, (b) EDC group, (c) GQDs group, and (d) EDC+GQDs group. (e)–(h) Are the merged differential interference contrast images (showing the optical density of the resin–dentin interface) and images acquired in green channel (showing enzymatic activity) in the control, EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs groups, respectively. D = dentin; HL = hybrid layer; A = adhesive; bar = 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

CLSM fluorescence images showing the in situ MMP zymography of the HL (pointers) at resin–dentin interfaces of different groups after thermocycling. (a) Control group, (b) EDC group, (c) GQDs group, and (d) EDC+GQDs group. (e)–(h) Are the merged differential interference contrast images (showing the optical density of the resin–dentin interface) and images acquired in green channel (showing enzymatic activity) in the control, EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs groups, respectively. D = dentin; HL = hybrid layer; A = adhesive; bar = 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Merged CLSM fluorescence image of the resin–dentin interface in the (a) control, (b) EDC, (c) GQDs, and (d) EDC+GQDs groups after bonding for 24 h during distilled water storage (immediate bonding interface). Red rhodamine fluorescence diffused from the adhesive to the HL of the binding interface and dentin tubules. Conversely, green fluorescein fluorescence diffused from dentin tubules to the HL of the bonding interface to indicate leakage. D = dentin; HL = hybrid layer; A = adhesive; bar = 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Merged CLSM fluorescence image of the resin–dentin interface in the (a) control, (b) EDC, (c) GQDs, and (d) EDC+GQDs groups after thermocycling. Red rhodamine fluorescence diffused from the adhesive to the HL of the binding interface and dentin tubules. Conversely, green fluorescein fluorescence diffused from dentin tubules to the HL of the binding interface to indicate leakage. The yellow light areas showed a generalized pattern of microleakage within the HL and adhesive layer, appearing as points of overlapping spectral light (yellow) in the emission profile of red (rhodamine B) and green (fluorescein) dyes. D = dentin; HL = hybrid layer; A = adhesive; bar = 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Schematic of GQDs and EDC interaction with collagen and inhibiting collagenase activity. (a) The interaction between GQDs and MMPs entrapped within the collage fibre. GQDs contain a functional group (carboxylic acid) capable of chelating the active-site catalytic zinc ions in the MMP molecule, hampered the activation of the catalytic domains within MMPs and preventing cleavage of the collage fibre. (b) The interaction between GQDs and collagen, carboxylic acids groups exist GQDs plane edges, which form noncovalent bonds (mainly hydrogen bonds) with collagen. (c) The interaction between GQDs and collagen with EDC addition, EDC activate the carboxyl groups (–COOH) at the plane edges of GQDs, which make GQDs form covalent bonds with collagen.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of graphene quantum dots and carbodiimide in promoting resin–dentin bond durability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resin-based dental adhesion is mostly utilized in minimally invasive operative dentistry. However, improving the durability and stability of resin–dentin bond interfaces remain a challenge. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) reinforced by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were introduced to modify the resin–dentin bond interfaces, thereby promoting their durability and stability.

GQDs, EDC, and EDC+GQDs groups were designed to evaluate the effects of GQDs and EDC on collagenase activity, the interaction of GQDs with collagen, and the resin–dentin interface. First, the effects of GQDs and EDC on collagenase activity was evaluated by Collagenase (EC 3.4.24.3) reacting with its substrate. The interaction of GQDs and EDC with collagen were evaluated by cross-linking degree analysis, sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and enzymatic hydrolysis. Second, the acid-etched and rinse adhesive system was used to evaluate the resin–dentin bond on the basis of microtensile bond strength, in situ zymography and fluorescence confocal laser scanning microscopy.

GQDs could inhibit collagenase activity. GQDs with the aid of EDC could cross-link collagen via covalent bonds and improve the anti-enzymatic hydrolysis of collagen. In the resin–dentin adhesion model, the μTBS of the EDC+GQDs group was significantly higher than the other control groups after thermocycling. The addition of EDC to GQDs could inhibit matrix metalloproteinase activity and promote the integrity of the bonding interfaces after thermocycling.

This study presents a novel strategy to modify the resin–dentin interface and provides a new application for GQDs. This strategy has the potential to improve the durability of resin-based restoration in dentistry.

ce:section.ce:para: Resin-based dental adhesive technology has been successfully used in minimally invasive operative dentistry. However, the durability and stability of resin–dentin bond interfaces remains questionable [1,2]. The failure of resin–dentin bond interface is mainly caused by the deterioration of the hybrid layer (HL), particularly the degradation of collagen fibrils [3,4]. For an etching–rinse system, a decreasing gradient of resin monomer diffusion within the acid-etched dentin results in incompletely infiltrated zones along the bottom of the HLs that contain denuded collagen fibrils [5]. This exposes the collagen fibrils to degradation by host-derived matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) within the dentin matrix [6–8] and cysteine cathepsins within the dentin matrix. A significant fall in bond strength of 36%–70% after 1 year of storage has been reported [9]. Therefore, improving the hydrolysis resistance of collagen fiber is one of the main research directions to improve the durability of dental bonding.

Natural collagen molecule self-assembles into a large and well-structured triple-helical fibrins with aldehyde alcohol condensation,aldehyde amine condensation, and aldehyde alcohol histidine cross-linking within and between collagen molecules [10,11,12]. The special helicoidal structure needs a high specific protease to be hydrolyzed (collagenases). However, after denaturation or unfolding, the role of other gelatinases has key importance in the HL stability. Based on this, researchers are developing theories and materials based on two approaches to preserve the collagen fibers in the HL. The first is protease inhibition. The use of competitive (or non-competitive) enzyme inhibitors promotes the formation of the enzyme-substrate complex that prevents the active site of the enzyme from hydrolyzing collagen substrate, such as the endogen collagen, chlorhexidine, galardin, and quaternary ammoniummethacrylates [13–15]. The second is to increases the collagen structural architecture by promoting collagen cross-linking, such as proanthocyanidins, carbodiimide, which inactivating the catalytic site of these enzymes, difficulting the access and posterior hydrolysis of collagenases and gelatinases [16–18].

As novel materials, graphene and its derivatives have been used in dentistry, such as titanium dental implants [19], membranes for bone regeneration [20], resins, cements [21], adhesives [22], and tooth-whitening procedures [23]. Graphene is a monolayer of sp2 hybrid-bonded carbon atoms arranged to form a honeycomb structure [24], providing extremely high mechanical strength and modulus of elasticity, excellent electrical thermal conductivities, and remarkable optical properties [25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional graphene sheets with lateral sizes less than 100 nm [26]. GQDs present a similar layered structure to graphene, but their carbon sheets are heavily oxygenated with hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups on the basal plane [27]. Carbonyl/carboxylic acids facilitate the combination of oxygen-containing groups with specific functional groups on biomolecules or other polymers [28], and this process can be achieved either through covalent [29] (e.g., nucleophilic substitution) or noncovalent [30] (e.g., Van der Waals forces, electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding) methods and be used for modifying materials. Therefore, carboxyl groups in GQDs may be combined with amino groups in collagen fibers.

1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) is a common cross-linking agent with low toxicity and good compatibility [31]. It is not incorporated directly into the cross-linked collagen, but it activates carboxylic acid groups to react with amino group to form an amido bond [32–34]. Therefore, EDC may allow carboxyl groups in GQDs to bind to amino groups of collagen.

In the present study, we explored whether GQDs with EDC have the ability to cross-link collagen fiber and inhibit the activity of MMPs, and we constructed a new adhesive protocol using GQDs with EDC to improve the stability of dentin bonding interface and improve the durability of dentin adhesion.

GQDs were purchased from Xiwang Technology (Shanghai, China). Acid-soluble collagen type I with a concentration of 3.52 mg/mL in 0.02 M acetic acid solution (Corning Incorporated, New York) was extracted from the rat tail tendon. The highly purified collagenase type I (Collagenase (EC 3.4.24.3), also named as Clostridiopeptidase A. or Clostridium histolyticum colagenase, or Collagenase A. or Collagenase I.) from Clostridium histolyticum was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Micro hydroxyproline (HYP) content assay kit was purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Collagenase activity colorimetric assay kit was purchased from Biovision (San Francisco, USA). EDC and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma Chemical (St. Louis, MO, USA). Universal adhesive and Filtek™ P60 post-tooth composite resin were obtained from 3 M (St. Paul, MN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of collagen cross-linker solutions and experimental design
ce:section.ce:para: Three types of collagen cross-linker solutions were prepared by using anhydrous ethanol as solvent to obtain 4 mg/mL of EDC, 5 mg/mL of GQDs, and 4 mg/mL of EDC + 5 mg/mL GQDs. Each solution containing EDC was added with NHS (1.28 mg/mL) to keep the EDC stable.

In the present study, the experimental groups were classified as the EDC group, GQDs group, EDC+GQDs group by using the above cross-linker to cross-link collagen. Accordingly, the control group comprised collagen without using the above cross-linker and was only treated with equal amount of solvent of anhydrous ethanol.

Each cross-linker solution was added with 3.52 mg/mL of collagen solution at a volume ratio of 1:1. The control groups of non-cross-linked collagen solution were added with the same amount of anhydrous ethanol. Each group was defined as EDC group, GQDs group, EDC+GQDs group, and control group. All samples were stored under room temperature for 24 h to obtain the modified collagen solution.

The collagen film was obtained by adding 500 μL of collagen solution (3.52 mg/mL) to each well of a clean 24-well plate and freeze–drying for 48 h (FreeZone 2.5 Plus, LABCONCO). Then, 500 μL of each cross-linker (EDC group, GQDs group, EDC+GQDs group) was placed into the collagen film of each well and cross-linked for 24 h at room temperature. For the control group, 500 μL of anhydrous ethanol was added. After the cross-linking was completed, the cross-linked collagen film was repeatedly rinsed with anhydrous ethanol, and then lyophilized (K850, Emitech Ltd., UK) to obtain the cross-linked collagen films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the cross-linked collagen solution and film

ce:section.ce:para:
Ninhydrin assay was used to evaluate the cross-linking degree of every group. Ninhydrin (2,2-dihydroxy-1,3-indanedione) was used to determine the amount of amino groups of each cross-linked film [35]. Each group of collagen film was immersed in 1 mL of ninhydrin coloration solution (85 mg of ninhydrin and 15 mg of hydrindantin dissolved in 10 mL of 2-methoxyethanol) and reacted at 100 °C for 20 min. The optical absorbance was then recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan) at 570 nm. The standard curves were calculated using different concentrations of glycine. The cross-linking degree was calculated as follows:
Cross-linking degree (%) = 100 (M0 − Mt)/M0,
where M0 represents the content of free amino groups in the untreated collagen film and Mt represents the content of free amino groups in the cross-linked collagen film. Each group comprised 10 samples (n = 10).

SDS–PAGE was used to evaluate the cross-linking interactions between GQDs and collagen solution [36]. Each 50 μL cross-linked solution (EDC, GQDs, EDC+GQDs, control group) was mixed with 10 μL of protein buffer (SDS–PAGE protein loading buffer) and heated at 98 °C for 10 min. For each group, 10 μL of samples was added into the sample tank for electrophoresis. At the end of electrophoresis, the plate was stained with 0.25% Coomassie bright blue solution for 2 h at room temperature, the dye was poured out, and the decolorizing solution of Coomassie bright blue was added. When the gel plate was clean and the background was clear, the gel was carefully transferred to the gel analysis system. An ultrahigh- sensitivity chemiluminescence imaging system (ChemiDoc XRS+, Bio-Rad) was used to capture photos for analysis.This experiment was repeated for three times in each group（n=3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Non-cross-linked (control group) and cross-linked (EDC, GQDs, EDC+GQDs) collagen films were detected through ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS50) from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 to evaluate the interaction of molecular groups between GQDs and collagen. Each group comprised three collagen film samples.

Collagenase was prepared by adding lyophilized powder of clostridiopeptidase A in an incubation medium containing zwitterionic organic chemical buffering agent to obtain a concentration of 100 U/mL. Every group of collagen film was immersed in 1 mL of 100 U/mL type I collagenase solution for 24 h (37 °C). After enzymatic hydrolysis, the above samples were centrifugated at 10,000 rpm/min for 10 min, the supernatant was taken, and 1 mL of 6 M HCL was added for hydrolysis at 98 °C for 24 h [37]. After the treatment, NaOH (6 M) solution was used to adjust the solution to neutral, and deionized water was added to reach a volume of 6 mL. Finally, HYP was detected using a micro HYP content assay kit (Solarbio, Beijing, China) in accordance with the manufacturer’s protocol. Optical absorbance was recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan) at 560 nm by using various known concentrations of HYP as the standard. The relative enzymolysis degree was calculated as follows:
Relative enzymolysis degree (%) = 100Ct/C0,
where C0 is the HYP concentration after the enzymolysis of the non-cross-linked collagen film and Ct is the HYP concentration after the enzymolysis of the cross-linked collagen film. Each group comprised 10 samples (n = 10).

Collagenase (EC 3.4.24.3) is an enzyme in the MMP family [38], which can break down collagen and assist the degradation of extracellular matrix. BioVision’s collagenase activity assay kit measures collagenase activity using a synthetic peptide (FALGPA) that mimics the structure of collagen. After lysis by collagenase, the synthesized peptide released luciferin, and the collagenase activity was calculated by measuring OD345 at different time points. Detection using BioVision’s collagenase activity assay kit was conducted in accordance with the manufacturer’s protocol. This assay was performed in a 96-well plate, and five wells were replicated for each group (n = 5). The experimental steps are as follows:
Group 1: 2 μL of EDC + 10 μL of collagenase (0.35 U/mL) + 88 μL of collagenase assay buffer
Group 2: 2 μL of GQDs + 10 μL of collagenase (0.35 U/mL) + 88 μL of collagenase assay buffer
Group 3: 2 μL of (EDC+GQDs) + 10 μL of collagenase (0.35 U/mL) + 88 μL of collagenase assay buffer
Group 4: (negative control group): 2 μL of anhydrous ethanol (solvent for cross-linker) + 10 μL of collagenase (0.35 U/mL) + 88 μL of collagenase assay buffer
Group 5: (positive control group): 2 μL of inhibitor (1,10-phenanthroline) + 10 μL of collagenase (0.35 U/mL) + 88 μL of collagenase assay buffer
Group 6: (background control): 100 μL of collagenase assay buffer

After mixing, all samples incubate at room temperature for 10 min. Each well was sucked out 60 μL, and 40 μL of collagenase substrate (FALGPA) was added and mixed. The absorbance was immediately read kinetically at OD345 in a microplate reader at 37 °C for 5–15 min (MQX200, Bio-Tek, USA). The absorbance (A345nm1 and A345nm2) was obtained at two time points (T1 and T2) in the linear range. The collagenase activity of the sample was calculated as follows:

Eighty extracted human third molars were obtained from Hospital of Stomatology affiliated to Anhui Medical University with patients informed consent. The research protocol was approved by the independent ethics committee of Anhui Medical University (document No. 20190234). . The teeth were stored at 4 °C in 0.5% chloramine T solution. Under the cooling of running water, the flat surfaces of middle/deep dentin were exposed with a low-speed diamond saw. A 600-grit silicon carbide (SiC) paper under cooling water was polished to simulate the production of smear layer [39]. After ultrasonic cleaning and drying, Smear layer-covered dentin surfaces were etched with 37% phosphoric acid for 15 s (etching gel, 3 M ESPE) and then rinsed. The surfaces were blot-dried according to the wet bonding technique and randomly divided into one of four groups: EDC, GQDs, EDC+GQDs, and control group. In each group, the dentin surface was scrubbed with 4 mg/mL of EDC, 5 mg/mL of GQDs, and 4 mg/mL of EDC + 5 mg/mL GQDs, and anhydrous ethanol for 60 s, respectively. The obtained sample was bonded with Adper™ Single Bond 2 (3 M, St. Paul, MN) in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. After each adhesive was lightly cured, 5 mm-thick composite resin (Filtek™ P60 3 M, St. Paul, MN) was built incrementally (1 mm) and separately polymerized for 40 s with an LED device (Bluephase 20i, Ivoclar Vivadent, Schaan, Liechtenstein). The composite bonded teeth were stored in distilled water at 37 °C for 24 h. Resin–dentin sticks with a cross-sectional area of approximately 1 mm2 were obtained in accordance with a previously described method [40]. Each stick was measured and recorded for bond strength calculation. Sticks were divided in two equal groups. The μTBS test was conducted immediately for the beams in the first subgroup to obtain the immediate bonding strength. The second subgroup was subjected to thermocycling before the μTBS test. Thermocycling was performed for 10,000 cycles by placing the beams in temperature cycling chambers (HUAN-S, Wuhan, China) at 5 °C – 55 °C with a dwell time of 30 s [41]. For the μTBS test, each stick was attached to a testing jig with cyanoacrylate adhesive and stressed to failure under tension with a digital universal testing machine (AGS-X, Shimadzu, Japan) using a crosshead speed of 1 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ zymography of the HL
ce:section.ce:para: In situ zymography fluorescence technique was used for examining MMP activities within HLs and around the dentin after resin–dentin bonding was performed in accordance with the above method. Forty additional teeth were processed for the in situ zymography. One-millimeter-thick disks of middle/deep coronal dentin were prepared and randomly divided into four groups: control, EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs group. The dental disk was treated with the adhesive systems as described for μTBS test. A 1-mm-thick flowable composite (Filtek™ P60 3 M, St. Paul, MN) was applied on the bonded disks and light-cured. Each group was divided in two equal subgroups (n = 5). The first subgroup was stored in distilled water for 24 h, and the second subgroup was subjected to thermocycling as described above before the test. After these procedures, each bonded sample was sectioned vertically into two equal parts to expose the resin–dentin interface, and the marginal part was removed. The two central slabs of each tooth were selected, yielding 10 slabs per subgroup for microscopy examination. Each slab was attached to a glass slide with cyanoacrylate glue and polished to approximately 50 μm thick for the in situ zymography test. All the cutting and polishing procedures were performed under water cooling. In the test, quenched fluorescein-conjugated gelatin (E-12055, Molecular Probes, Eugene, OR, USA) was used as the substrate for the activated endogenous MMPs in the partially demineralized dentin [42]. A 1.0 mg/mL stock solution of self-quenched fluorescein-conjugated gelatin was first prepared. The gelatin stock solution was added to an anti-fading agent (Mounting Medium with DAPI H-1200, Vectashield, Vector Laboratories LTD, Cambridgeshire, UK) and diluted to 1:1:8 with dilution buffer (pH 8.0). Fifty microliters of quenched fluorescein-conjugated gelatin was then pipetted on the surface of each resin–dentin bonding slab prepared and protected with a glass cover slip. The glass slides were incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 24 h in a humidity chamber. Green fluorescence (λex/λem = 488/530 nm) released after gelatin hydrolysis by the MMPs was imaged with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM; LSM-880, Carl Zeiss). Optical sections with a thickness of 85 μm were acquired from different focal planes, and the stacked images were analyzed and processed with ZEN software (ZEN2.3, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the micropermeability and nanoleakage of the resin–dentin interfaces using double fluorescence technique
ce:section.ce:para: Double fluorescence technique with CLSM was used to assess the interfacial morphology of the resin–dentin interdiffusion zone and the distribution of dentin bonding agents [43]. Twenty-four tooth specimens were randomly divided into four groups: control, EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs group. The tooth crown was cut transversally to expose the pulp chamber. The enamel was removed, and the dentin near the pulp chamber was polished. Resin bonding was performed as mentioned above, except that the adhesive was premixed with 0.05 wt% rhodamine B (Solarbio, Beijing, China) to show red fluorescence prior to adhesive application. Each group was divided into two equal subgroups (n = 3). For the first subgroup (immediate group), the exposed pulp chamber was etched for 20 s with 37% phosphoric acid and then filled with 0.1 wt% aqueous fluorescein solution (Solarbio, Beijing, China) to show green fluorescence for 3 h after resin bonding. The fluorescein infiltrated from the pulp chamber through the dentinal tubules into the resin–dentin bond surface, an opposite direction compared with rhodamine infiltration. For the second subgroup (thermocycling group), after resin bonding, the specimens were subjected to thermocycling as described above. Then, the pulp chamber was etched and filled with fluorescein solution for 3 h similar to the first subgroup. Thereafter, specimens of both subgroups were copiously rinsed with water and treated in an ultrasonic water bath for 2 min. Each specimen was cut vertically to expose the resin–dentin interface and polished using an ascending grit of SiC abrasive papers. Then, the specimens were ultrasonically cleaned (5 min) [44]. Fluorescein was activated by green fluorescence (λex/λem = 488/530 nm), whereas rhodamine B was activated by red fluorescence (λex/λem = 540/590 nm). Analysis of the bonded interfaces was performed by using a dye-assisted CLSM (LSM-880, Carl Zeiss) equipped with a 63×/1.4 NA oil immersion lens.

Parametric statistical methods were used after validating the normality and homoscedasticity assumptions of the corresponding datasets. One-Way ANOVA and Holm-Šídák pairwise comparison procedures were used to compare the cross-linking degree, relative enzymolysis degree and inhibition of collagenase activity among all the groups. Two-Way ANOVA (variables: groups and challenge by thermocycling) was used to analyse the μTBS data. However, the interaction of the two factors was not significant (p>0.05), so the interaction between the two is not considered in the Two-Way ANOVA model, and one-way ANOVA model was performed. The analyses were performed using SPSS 22.0. For all analyses, statistical significance was preset at α = 0.05.

The cross-linking degree of the cross-linker with collagen in each group is shown in Table 1 . The cross-linking degree of the cross-linking agent with collagen among the groups was different (p < 0.05). The cross-linking degree of the GQDs group was significantly lower than that of the EDC or EDC+GQDs group (p < 0.05). However, the cross-linking degree of the EDC group was slightly lower than that of the EDC+GQDs group (p < 0.05).

The SDS–PAGE results after cross-linking for each group are shown in Fig. 1 . The electrophoresis bands of the GQDs and control groups showed no difference. Therefore, the results of the GQDs group indicated that the molecular weight of collagen did not change after cross-linking under electrophoresis conditions. In the EDC and EDC+GQDs groups, no protein band was observed in the collagen swimming lane after cross-linking, indicating that the molecular weight of collagen in these two groups increased significantly and could not enter the gel pore.

The FTIR spectra of collagen modified with EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The ATR-FTIR spectrum of the non-cross-linking collagen was characterized by four band regions: 3321 cm−1 (amide A, representative of OH and NH stretching), 3077 cm−1 (amide B, representative of CH and NH3 + stretching), 1652 cm−1 (amide I, representative of CO stretching and NH bending), and 1557 cm−1 (amide II, representative of NH bending and CN stretching). Collagen displays distinct amide bands via FTIR, which can provide information on its triple helix structure [45,46]. The changes in the secondary structure of collagen and the molecular interactions between collagen with EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs were investigated by examining four band regions.

The resisting enzymatic hydrolysisability of collagen after cross-linking with different cross-linkers was determined by measuring the content of free HYP in each group while the cross-linked collagen film was exposed to collagenase. The relative enzymolysis degrees of collagen films cross-linked by different cross-linkers are shown in Table 2 . The lower the relative enzymatic hydrolysis degree, the stronger the resisting enzymatic hydrolysis effect of each group of cross-linker-modified collagen. The relative enzymatic hydrolysis degree of the GQDs group was the highest, followed by the EDC group and EDC+GQDs group had the lowest relative enzymatic hydrolysis degree. There were significant differences between relative enzymatic hydrolysis of the three groups (p < 0.05). The results showed that the resisting enzymatic hydrolysis effect of the EDC+GQDs group was the strongest.

The inhibition of collagenase activity by different treatment agents is given in Table 3 . The collagenase activity of the negative control group was significantly higher than that of the other groups, which showed that the positive control, EDC, GQDS, and EDC+GQDs groups all had inhibitory effects on collagenase activity. The positive control group had the lowest collagenase activity and the strongest inhibitory effect. The collagenase activity of the EDC group was higher than that of the GQDs group, and the EDC+GQDs group showed the weakest collagenase activity in the three experimental groups. The collagenase activity of the GQDs group was higher than that of the EDC group, indicating that GQDs had stronger inhibitory effect on collagenase activity than EDC. A significant difference in five groups was found (p < 0.05).

The μTBS (MPa) of each group is shown in Table 4 . No statistical difference was observed between the experimental group and the control group in the immediate μTBS (p > 0.05). After thermocycling, the μTBS of the four groups decreased to different degrees and showed statistically significant loss of bond strength compared to immediate (p < 0.05). Specimens of the control group showed a 36.54% decrease in bond strength. No statistically significant differences in μTBS were observed among the EDC, GQDs, and control groups after thermocycling (p > 0.05). While the specimens of the EDC+GQDs group showed a 12.98% decrease in bond strength after thermocycling, the EDC+GQDs group showed significant differences in μTBS compared with the other three groups (p < 0.05). This finding indicated that GQDs + EDC treatment of the dentin collagen matrix could inhibit the aging of resin–dentin interfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ MMP zymography of the HL
ce:section.ce:para:
The MMP activities within HLs of resin–dentin bonding interface were observed by in situ zymography fluorescence technique. In this technique, activated endogenous MMPs in the partially demineralized dentin will hydrolyze the substrate of self-quenched fluorescein-conjugated gelatin to release fluorescein. Thus, the strength of the fluorescence image represented the activity of endogenous MMPs. The representative images of the four groups for immediate bonding interface and for bonding interface after thermomechanical cycling are shown in Figs. 3 and 4
, respectively.

In Fig. 3, in situ zymography revealed an intense green fluorescence within the HL and around the dentinal tubules in the control group, indicating that the fluorescein-conjugated gelatin was strongly hydrolyzed at these sites (Fig. 3a and e). The EDC (Fig. 3b and f), GQDs (Fig. 3c and g), and EDC+GQDs group (Fig. 3d and h) showed almost no green fluorescence within the HL zone, indicating strong inhibition of MMP activity.

In Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity in all groups increased after thermomechanical cycling compared with the immediate resin–dentin interface (Fig. 3), indicating that thermomechanical cycling results in the breakage of the bonding interface to some extent, which exposes more endogenous MMPs. However, the control (Fig. 4a and e) and EDC groups (Fig. 4b and f) showed significantly increased MMP activity in the HL and dentinal tubules after aging compared with the GQDs (Fig. 4c and g) and EDC+GQDs groups (Fig. 4d and e). In the GQDs group, some green fluorescence at the bottom of the HL was also found after thermomechanical cycling (Fig. 4c and g); however, the MMP activity remained almost the same in the EDC+GQDs group before and after thermomechanical cycling (Figs. 3d, h, 4d and h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CLSM evaluation of the micropermeability of resin–dentin interfaces
ce:section.ce:para:
In the experiment, rhodamine to show red fluorescence was pre-mixed with the adhesive, which diffused from the adhesive to the HL of bonding interface and the dentin tubules. After resin bonding, the pulp chamber was filled with fluorescein (to show green fluorescence). Fluorescein diffused from the pulp chamber to the dentin tubules and HL of the bonding interface, an opposite direction compared with rhodamine diffusion. Therefore, by merging the double fluorescence image, the micropermeability characteristics of resin–dentin interfaces can be easily determined. Figs. 5 and 6
show the immediate interface and thermomechanical cycling interface exhibiting the micropermeability characteristics of resin–dentin interfaces created by the four groups, respectively. No micropermeability or gap formation at the HL interface was present in the four groups after bonding formation for 24 h (Fig. 5). After thermocycling, the specimens in the control, EDC, and GQDs groups in double fluorescence examination, a rhodamine B-labeled HL completely affected by fluorescein penetration (micropermeability)) through the porous resin–dentin interface, showing the yellow fluorescence (Fig. 6a–c). The specimens in the EDC+GQDs group showed no signs of micropermeability) at the HL interface of resin–dentin (Fig. 6d).

Cross-linking the exposed collagen matrix in the HL and inhibiting the activity of endogenous MMPs that are entrapped within the collage matrix are an important strategy to stabilize the resin–dentin bonding interface to achieve adhesive durability [47–49]. Recently, GQDs as graphene derivatives have drawn considerable attention due to their unique properties. The lateral dimension of GQDs ranged from 2 nm to 6 nm, and the space between the collagen fibrils (interfibrillar space) is about 30 ± 11 nm [50]. Therefore, GQDs can completely enter the collagen fiber. GQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carbonyl/carboxylic acids groups, on the plane edges, allowing them to interact with collagen.

In the present study, the cross-linking degree (Table 1) and ATR-FTIR spectra (Fig. 2) showed that GQDs could interact with cross-linked collagen molecules. In the cross-linking degree experiment, the cross-linking ability of the cross-linker was determined by the content of residual free amino groups after the interaction of cross-linker with collagen [36]. FTIR also confirmed the changes in amino groupsThe absorption in the amide I region is the most useful for infrared spectroscopic analysis of the secondary structure of proteins, and its exact location depends on the conformation of the protein structure. Comparing the changes in the absorption in the amide I region, Fig. 2 shows that the main absorption bands in the amide I region remain almost in the same position with slight changes after cross-linking, suggesting that the collagen triple helix in the film can be retained after cross-linking. The comparison of the changes in the position of collagen characteristic peaks revealed that all of the cross-linkers could bind to collagen molecules. By comparing the changes in the position and strength of the characteristic peaks of collagen, the characteristic peaks of amide A, I, and II were transferred to lower wavenumbers (Fig. 2), indicating the hydrogen bond interaction between the cross-linking agent and collagen. By comparing the changes in the strength of the characteristic peaks of collagen, the cross-linking collagen samples have lower intensity N-H stretching (amide A) and higher N–H bending bands (amide I), indicating the formation of a new cross-linking [51,52,53,54].

Therefore, GQDs could interact with the amino groups of the collagen molecule, but the cross-linking mechanism remains to be fully elucidated. SDS–PAGE was performed to determine whether collagen was cross-linked by noncovalent or covalent bonds in GQDs. SDS is a strong anionic detergent that can break hydrogen bonds within and between molecules, causing molecules to unfold and destroy the secondary and tertiary structures of protein molecules. The mobility of collagen molecules in the gel is related to the molecular weight of collagen but is not affected by the original charge and shape of collagen. The SDS-PAGE results showed that the electrophoretic bands of the GQDs group and the unmodified collagen had identical protein band in swimming lanes, and the EDC and EDC+GQDs groups had no swimming band. The results suggested that EDC and EDC+GODs treated collagen molecule couldn’t pass through the gel molecular sieve (the size of gel pores), whereas GODs treated collagen could pass through. Therefore, we concluded that SDS may break the interaction between the collagen and GODs molecule and can’t break the interaction between the collagen and EDC or EDC+GODs molecule. To further determined the molecule interaction, we also carried out the Native-PAGE test in which no SDS was added into the gel. The results showed that there were no swimming protein band in the electrophoresis lane for all the four groups of control group, GODs group, EDC group, and EDC+GODs group (Seeing supporting imformation), which implied that collagen and cross-linked collagen molecule without SDS interaction were larger than the the gel molecular sieve (the size of gel pores). Therefore, from the results of SDS-PAGE and Native-PAGE, we may propose that SDS break the noncovalent bonds between GQDs and collagen, and also the noncovalent bonds within the well-structured triple-helical collagen fibrins. It implied that that noncovalent bonds might exist between GQDs and collagen molecule. Of cause, it needs further study to verify it. The noncovalent interaction between GQDs and collagen is mainly through supramolecular interactions including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and π–π stacking between the highly oxygen functional groups (carboxylic acids and hydroxyl) of GQDs and the amine groups present in collagen [28]. The cross-linked collagen film of GQDs showed a slight resisting enzymatic hydrolysisability, which corresponded to the cross-linking effect.

GQDs also showed a relatively strong inhibitory effect on collagenase activity (Table 3). MMPs are a class of zinc- and calcium-dependent endopeptidases [51]. The main inhibitory mechanism of MMPs is based on divalent metal ion chelation. Synthetic MMP inhibitors often contain a functional group (e.g., carboxylic acid, hydroxamic acid, sulfhydryl, phosphinils, thiols) capable of chelating the active-site catalytic zinc ions in the MMP molecule, preventing the bonding and cleavage of the substrate [52]. The sequestration of calcium and zinc ions hampers the activation of the catalytic domains within MMPs [55]. Therefore, the inhibitory effect of GQDs on MMP activation may be related to the carboxyl groups at the edge of the GQD sheet chelate active-site catalytic zinc ions in the MMP molecule, thereby inhibiting the activation of the catalytic domains of MMPsAnd also, GQDs can completely enter the collagen fibrils, which may make the collagenase to access active site difficulty.
Therefore, GQDs may cross-link collagen via noncovalent bonds and inhibit collagenase by chelating active-site catalytic mental zinc ions in the MMP molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic action of GQDs and EDC in inhibiting collagen degradation
ce:section.ce:para: EDC can activate carboxyl groups to react with amine groups to form an amido bond; thus, it is frequently used as a cross-linker to cross-link protein due to its good biocompatibility [56]. The cross-linking takes place due to the reaction between the carboxyl groups of glutamic/aspartic acid residues and the amine groups of lysine of collagen, thereby forming amide bonds. EDC activates the side chain COOH group of aspartic and glutamic acid, followed by aminolysis of the O-isoacylurea intermediates by NH2 groups of (hydroxyl) lysine [57] to induce the formation of an amide bond, which leads to the formation of intra- and inter-helical cross-links. In the study, no protein band was found in the swimming lanes of the EDC group, indicating that covalent bonds formed between EDC and collagen (Fig. 1). The results of cross-linking degree and anti-enzymatic hydrolysis also confirmed EDC’s cross-linking effect.

As discussed above, EDC may activate the carboxyl groups (COOH) at the plane edges of GQDs, which allow them to easily react with the amine groups (NH2) of lysine and hydroxyproline to form amide bonds [29]. Thus, EDC may act as a bridge to cross-link collagen. No protein band was found in the swimming lanes of the EDC+GQDs group after electrophoresis, indicating that covalent bonds formed between collagen molecules (Fig. 1).

Compared with GQDs cross-linking collagen via noncovalent bonds, EDC and EDC with GQDs cross-link collagen via covalent bonds. Therefore, GQDs had a limited cross-linking effect, whereas the cross-linking effect of GQDs with EDC was significantly improved (Tables 1 and 2). The resistance enzymatic hydrolysis ability and inhibitory effect on MMP activation of the EDC+GQDs group were much stronger than those of the EDC or GQDs group (Tables 2 and 3). All of the above results suggested that EDC and GQDs had synergetic action in inhibiting collagen degradation.

The introduction of additional cross-links increases the mechanical strength of collagen fibers [58]. Increased intermolecular cross-links among the triple helical structures of collagen restrict the degrees of freedom of its α-chains, masking the cleavage site of collagen. Intermolecular cross-links are capable of improving the resistance of collagen against enzymatic degradation [59]. Additional cross-links may also be produced between collagen and proteolytic enzymes. Changing the 3D configuration of these enzymes results in masking of their collagen binding sites [60]. The addition of EDC to GQDs not only improved the cross-linking degree but also enhanced the aesistance enzymatic hydrolysis ability and inhibitory effect on MMP activation (Fig. 7 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Promotion of dentin bond stability of acid-etched and rinse adhesive systems by GQDs and EDC primer
ce:section.ce:para: A bonding model with an acid-etched and rinse adhesive system was used to evaluate the effects of GQDs on resin–dentin bonding strength and durability. Thermocycling is a commonly used thermal fatigue method to evaluate bond durability. It simulates the thermal changes that occur in the oral cavity caused by eating, drinking, and breathing. Such cycles might occur between 20 and 50 times in a day, reaching 10,000 cycles in a year [61]. In our experiment, 10,000 cold and heat cycles were performed on the resin–dentin bonding specimens, and this duration was equivalent to 1 year of prosthesis bonding in the oral cavity. The immediate μTBS results of each group showed no difference (p > 0.05). However, after thermocycling, the μTBS results of the control, EDC, and GQDs groups decreased significantly compared with their immediate μTBS (p < 0.05). However, the μTBS of the EDC+GQDs group decreased slightly. This may be due to the improved anti-enzymatic hydrolysis and inhibitory effect on MMP activation discussed above. Although EDC+GQDs slowed the decline of bond strength after aging, they did not completely block these phenomena (Table 4). The decline in the bond strength of the EDC+GQDs group may be related to the loss of integrity of resinous components within the HLs due to polymer swelling and resin leaching that occurs after water sorption.

The above experiment showed that GQDs and EDC can inhibit collagenase activity. However, collagenase is mandatory, homogenization, and the localization of enzyme activity was precluded. For this reason, we performed in situ zymography to determine the precise localization of MMP activity within the HL created by the acid-etched and rinsed adhesives. In situ zymography is, in fact, an adaptation of substrate zymography that does not require previous extraction of enzymes from the tissue. Hence, the analysis is made in situ [62]. The experimental results showed that little collagenase activity was detected at the bottom of the HL in the EDC, GQDs, and EDC+GQDs groups immediately after binding (Fig. 3). However, only the EDC+GQDs group showed little MMP activity at the bottom of the HL after aging (Fig. 4). The collagenase activity of the GQDs group increased significantly because noncovalently bound molecules may leach out of the HL. The instability of these MMP inhibitors compromises long-term anti-proteolytic benefits and results only in delaying and not preventing the degradation of adhesive interfaces. The collagenase activity of the EDC group increased significantly due to the loss of a cross-linker effect on collagenases after aging [63]. The covalent linkage between collagen and GQDs was promoted by the addition of EDC, which affects the long-term stability of MMPs, preventing the degradation of adhesive interfaces.

The micropermeability of resin–dentin bonded interface was evaluated using fluorescence CLSM. The adhesive doped with rhodamine B displayed a red fluorescence, and the filtration of fluorescein from the pulp chamber through dentinal tubules into the resin–dentin interface showed green fluorescence [44]. The two overlapping signals showed a yellow. For an ideal resin–dentin interface, there should be no overlapping of the two signals, which is indicative of adhesive permeation into the bottom of HLs. The results of each group showed no nanoleakage at the resin–dentin interfaces after bonding (Fig. 4), but only the EDC+GQDs group exhibited no obvious nanoleakage after thermomechanical cycling (Fig. 5). Although EDC and GQDs possessed anti-collagenolytic potential, dentin bonded with EDC or GQDs showed deterioration of the HLs after aging, which is consistent with the results of in situ zymography of the HL and μTBS above. EDC-coupled GQDs and collagen fibers formed stable covalent cross-linking, which increased the resistance to enzymatic hydrolysis of collagen fibers. On the other hand, it inhibited endogenous MMP activity and stabilized resin-infiltrated dentin matrices improved the durability of resin–tooth interfaces.

This study proved that GQDs may cross-link collagen via noncovalent bonds and have relatively strong ability to inhibit collagenase activity, but theresisting enzymatic hydrolysis ability is relatively limited and unstable. GQDs combined with EDC can form covalent linkage with collagen fibers, improving the ability of resisting enzymatic hydrolysis abilityof collagen fibers and inhibiting the activity of collagenase. GQDs with EDC as a primer using the acid-etched and rinse adhesive system, which may improve the bonding strength after thermocycling, inhibit MMP activity in situ and promote the integrity of the HL after thermocycling. Therefore, GQDs mediated by EDC can inhibit the hydrolysis of collagen fibers and improve the adhesion durability.
